data.title: Gaming and Leisure Properties: This Forgotten Casino REIT Has A Near 7% Jackpot Yield
data.description:
Introduction

When it comes to the casino REIT space, VICI Properties (VICI) gets all the attention, and for good reason. However, the regional casino REIT, Gaming and Leisure Properties (GLPI), is just as good in my opinion.

I've been quite bullish on the REIT sector (XLRE) for the past 3 years, mainly due to higher-for-longer interest rates and fears of inflation reaccelerating, causing long-term yields to spike.

I view this as a long-term buying opportunity and think GLPI is poised for solid upside in the years ahead. Especially if their recent earnings are any indication.

In this article, I discuss GLPI's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why I remain bullish on the stock.

Previous Thesis

I last covered Gaming and Leisure Properties back in June in an article titled: Sleep Well With This Cheap 6.6% Yield. Despite recessionary risks, the yield, valuation, and regional focus made the stock attractive amidst uncertainty.

Since, the REIT's share price has been somewhat volatile but flat since June. GLPI is down roughly 2% in comparison to the S&P (SP500), up roughly 14%.

During their Q2, FFO declined $0.04 year-over-year, a result of net income decreasing $9 million. But the dividend yield above 6% and double-digit upside to their price target of $55 made them appealing long-term.

Strong Quarter

During their most recent quarter, GLPI deployed $875 million at a blended cap rate of 9.3%. Management expects these to grow cash rents by 5%, while simultaneously strengthening their partnerships with two tenants.

Total income from real estate increased roughly $12 million, driven by a $20 million increase in cash rent. This was driven by the REIT's Bally's (BALY) acquisitions in Kansas City & Shreveport as well as the Chicago land lease.

The downtown Chicago project is for a permanent resort expected to have 3,300 slots, 170 gaming tables, 500 hotel rooms, multiple restaurants, and a 2-acre riverside park. This is slated to open in Q4 2026.

GLPI currently has 68 properties across 20 states. Relatively small, the REIT has plenty of room for growth and scale.

Since my last thesis, the REIT has announced several acquisitions. In September, they provided $225 million in financing for a 4-star casino resort in Healdsburg, California. This was at an interest rate of 12.79% and expected to be completed in the Summer of 2027.

They also acquired their second property in New Mexico for $183.75 million at a cap rate of 8.2%.

And lastly, GLPI acquired land at a cap rate of 8.2% and provided funding for the future site of Live! Casino and Hotel Virginia, located in Richmond. This is expected to open in late 2027.

All acquisitions are expected to be immediately accretive to their bottom line. Additionally, management stated they have a $3 billion pipeline. So, GLPI looks poised for strong growth in a lower interest rate environment. All their recent acquisitions and pipeline should result in solid top & bottom line growth in the years ahead.

Financials

As a result of their strong investment activity, GLPI's revenue grew 3.2% year-over-year despite missing analysts' estimates in the quarter. Revenue amounted to $397.6 million. FFO was up a notable $0.29 from the prior quarter, while AFFO also grew from the prior quarter and year.

AFFO was $0.97, comfortably exceeding the company's current dividend of $0.78. This gave the REIT a comfortable payout ratio of 80.4%. Leverage also ticked down to 4.4x from 4.7x in Q2 and 4.9x at the end of last year.

This was lower than VICI Properties, who also saw their leverage decrease to 5.0x. This gives GLPI ample room to increase leverage to fund future acquisitions, as well as continue covering the dividend should coverage become tight for unforeseen reasons. Something else that also puts GLPI in a comfortable position is that they have no debt maturing until 2027.

Management stated during earnings that they could fund their entire pipeline with debt, and this would only increase leverage to 5.1x. This was near the lower end of their targeted range of 5.0x–5.5x. Management smartly took advantage and raised capital to fund their pipeline. They sold shares at $48 for $363 million in forward equity.

Raised Guidance

Seeing their strong activity, it's no surprise GLPI raised its full-year AFFO guidance. AFFO is now expected in a range of $3.86 - $3.88, up a penny on the lower and higher ends. This gives them an annualized payout ratio of 80%, comfortable for a REIT. This also ensures their 6.8% yield will likely remain safe.

While I like to see REITs with more cushion and prefer payout ratios below 75%, an 80% payout ratio for a REIT is nothing that should alarm investors.

Attractive Upside

At $45.47 at the time of writing, GLPI's upside potential and nearly 7% dividend yield may be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors at a forward multiple of 11.75x.

This is currently lower than VICI's 12.95x and well below the sector median's 15.28x. For the near term, inflation worries, macro uncertainty, and treasury yield volatility will likely limit upside. But over the next 12 to 24 months, I can see the REIT breaking out above $50 and closer to their high price target.

Risks & Bottom Line

For casinos, even regional ones like Gaming and Leisure Properties, their portfolios are resilient, as seen by theirs and VICI's 100% rent collection during COVID. However, the uptick in job losses could be an indicator of the economy slowing.

In my opinion, I believe we have a 40% to 60% chance of seeing a recession in the next year or two. Especially if job losses continue creeping up.

If so, this will result in a slowdown at the REIT's properties, similar to what peer VICI Properties is currently dealing with. If so, this could cause price volatility and underperformance going forward.

But at a forward P/AFFO multiple of 11.75x, well-covered yield near 7%, and likelihood to see tailwinds from lower base rates, I maintain my buy rating on Gaming and Leisure Properties.

data.title: FLRN: A Fine Way To Play The Fed Staying At Current Rates
data.description:
The State Street® SPDR® Bloomberg Investment Grade Floating Rate ETF (FLRN) is an effectively zero-duration risk fixed income ETF that works with variable rate instruments, with very minor credit spreads. It's about in line other floating rate fixed income ETFs in terms of expense ratios at 0.15%.

Regarding the logic of playing the yield curve at this point with variable rate exposures, CPI is still running quite hot and an independent Fed will need to keep rates at these levels to address that in our view, which would trounce cutting bets. Expectations are also an issue. On the other hand, there is a major issue for the US balance sheet when debt rolls over at higher rates, where cuts would be a major help. Still we are inclined to index away from duration after a cut has already taken place in the form of a reduction at the end of October in the Fed funds rate target. Another jobs report has been canceled even with the possible end of the shutdown, so we are still waiting to see what the economic data is pointing to though private data sources are showing that it could be time for recession bets which would once again support a rate cutting narrative. AI is an important factor in the job dynamics, and we'd venture that it can continue for quite a long time, particularly as trends of cheaper to train and deploy AI materialises.

ETF Breakdown

There are two effects running counter to each other in speculating on the yield curve. Should cuts not continue something like FLRN, particularly with the additional YTM brought by the credit spreads to counteract the expense ratio, is a good option - otherwise indexing to duration is the way to play fixed income. On one hand there are the CPI considerations, which is one of the few data that still came out during the shut down. The issue with inflation is that if expectations anchor at higher levels, the inflation battle is usually lost definitively unless a major economic paradigm shift, such as a recession, occurs.

Job cuts are up dramatically YoY and QoQ based on data from outplacement and hiring firms. This would be the case for rate cutting coming back though the data should be considered a bit anecdotal and also coming from private sources as the government shut down prevents official national data from being collected and published in the US. Other effects supporting a rate cut narrative would be fiscal policy changes that hit disposable incomes of lower income households more under Trump, reversing the "stimulus-check economy" of the early 2020s following and including the COVID-19 period. While it isn't technically in the Fed's formal mandate, they may be responsive to the economic effects that fiscal pressure might have on future government spending due to the still considerable room for increases in debt costs for the US government once debt rolls over. There is an important maturity wall in 2026 of debt that was issued at really low rates from before. Some of these considerations, particularly a recognition of job market pressure perhaps even from the AI adoption factor alone, likely fueled the latest Fed decision.

However, based on the upward shifts of the yield curve in the short term there is clearly a cognizance of persisting inflation with the latest CPI figure that is a little more updated than most other government data remaining hot, reflecting still to-be-digested tariff effects and continued pass through of those higher costs to consumers despite the shakier ground that they are on. The Fed may not consider more tenuous connections between US government balances and government spending as it would regard the growth mandate, and it seems unlikely that the FOMC would fold to pressure from Trump, where independence of the Fed has been an important trading narrative, with downside to the USD should they prove partial to political cycles. Indeed, inflation expectations are up according to some surveys, which is the problem and the unacceptable risk to the Fed of leaving inflation unaddressed. Inflation can anchor at higher levels and become very difficult to fix. They did resolve to reduce the Fed funds rate target once again in the latest meeting, but markets seem to be adjusting their expectations upwards for rates with questions about how deep rate cuts will actually be able to go.

Bottom Line

The conclusion is that markets are less convinced of as many cuts compared to a month ago. We'd agree with this notion, and with CPI being of the few more updated data, we'd play away from duration bets and consider caution with something that produces a spot return for top-rated investment grade exposures in the US. For foreign investors, there would also be money to be made on a US recovery in the event that rates don't get cut as much as expected.

data.title: American Battery Technology Has A Demand Tailwind For Commercial Transition
data.description:
American Battery Technology Company (ABAT) is transitioning its business from research and development to commercial operations, with strong demand drivers for Lithium in its favor.

Introduction to American Battery Technology

American Battery Technology Company, or ABTC, is a Reno-based lithium-ion battery recycling and metal extraction company that utilizes proprietary methods to recycle batteries and extract valuable raw materials from them. The company was listed on the Nasdaq in September 2023 and was initially supported by grants. Growing commercial opportunities and a 30k tonne per-year lithium mine are pushing the company into new territory.

ABTC Celebrates a Multi-Million-Dollar Award

ABTC was in the news last week after being awarded the “largest lithium-ion battery cleanup” deal in history from the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).

The contract is related to a January 16 fire at a 300-megawatt battery storage facility in Monterey, California. The premises were owned by the Texas power firm Vistra, which contained approximately 100,000 lithium-ion battery modules, 55% of which were damaged in the fire, according to the EPA.

Alongside the cleanup process, it will allow materials to re-enter the domestic battery supply chain, with ABTC able to extract lithium, cobalt, and nickel from the battery pile. The project will lead to around $30 million of recoverable materials for ABTC at today's prices.

That could lead to valuable raw materials becoming available to ABTC.

ABTC is Transitioning to Commercial Operations

Much of ABTC’s early life was driven by grants for research & development efforts. The company now has a portfolio of partnerships with brands such as Ford, Chrysler, Stellantis, and General Motors.

The move to commercial operations has started strongly, with ABTC’s full-year earnings release showing a 180% increase in quarterly revenue.

The full year 2025 Revenue surged by 1,149% to $4.3 million, from $0.3 million in 2024. The initial drawback is that costs are high due to expansion. However, the company reduced its expenses by 30% to $31.4 million over the year.

However, there are tangible results to be seen in capacity, with a 70% throughput increase from Q3 to Q4.

In the most recent quarter, ABTC was able to expand its cash and restricted cash to $30.9 million, compared to $12.5 million. The overall cash balance was $55.6 million, and the company removed all convertible notes to secure a position of zero long-term debt. The recent battery clean-up award will also generate approximately $30 million at current prices, as I noted.

The jewel in the crown is the company’s Tonopah Flats Lithium Project, which has a mine life of 45 years and can produce 30,000 tonnes of lithium per year. The project has an after-tax Net Present Value at 8% of $2.57 billion and an Internal Rate of Return of 21.8%.

ABTC is also seeing new business from the buildout of artificial intelligence data centers.

“In support of the rapid U.S. buildout of data centers for artificial intelligence, machine learning, and cybersecurity applications, ABTC’s recycling facility is now receiving substantial quantities of feed material for recycling from stationary battery energy storage systems,” a statement said.

That buildout is anticipated to continue in the coming years and should continue to support revenue and partnership opportunities.

ABTC’s valuation is currently high at 13x sales on a forward basis. That should be seen as temporary with the company’s growing commercial focus and the opening of the Tonopah Flats mine.

Supply and demand constraints align with geopolitical stress. American Battery Technology provides exposure to commodities that are becoming very valuable to the global supply chain in mobile phones, electric vehicles, and other technologies.

Much of the tension surrounding the U.S. and China trade talks recently has surrounded China’s dominance in rare earth materials. The current U.S. administration has been very active in securing supply chains in the country, with a focus on building semiconductor plants and even taking shareholding stakes in companies.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said last week that the country is moving at “warp speed” to build independent supply chains. Technology commodities are becoming the new crude oil, and ABTC could have a long-term future in salvaging used batteries.

Another driver of future success can come from advances in battery technology. Technology in batteries moves ahead very quickly, as we have seen with faster cell phone battery charging and EV charging capabilities.

A recent article from Wired highlighted some potential battery breakthroughs for the future and said:

“The big deal battery breakthroughs all have something in common: They’re related to the lithium-ion battery.”

“Lithium-ion is already very mature,” says Stoikou. Lots of players have invested big money in the technology, so “any new one is going to have to compete with the status quo,” they added.

Lithium-ion has solidified its place as the dominant technology and should keep a floor under demand, especially for a horizon of 5-10 years. ABTC says on its home page that “less than 1% of the global manufacturing capacity of each of the primary battery metals (lithium, nickel, cobalt, and manganese) is currently within the US. The federal government considers all four minerals to be ‘essential to the economic or national security of the US.’”

Risks to the Investment Thesis

The company is still in the early stages of its commercial plans, and the valuation is high. That looks only at future mining revenue, but investors should factor it into their holding size. The company is seeing strong revenue gains but is not yet profitable, and we will need to see costs coming down to meet higher revenue. A slowing in expansion-related costs is likely in the coming quarters.

Conclusion

American Battery Technology Co. has not been making a lot of headlines due to its financial status. However, a move to commercial operations could see the company become an important part of the commodity ecosystem. Lithium battery demand will continue to be on the upswing, and rapid advances will provide materials for ABTC to harvest. The arrival of the company’s mining facility looks set to be a game-changer for the company.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Alibaba's Qwen Is Coming For ChatGPT
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Alibaba (BABA) reportedly preps revamp of AI app to resemble ChatGPT. (00:22) China's Baidu (BIDU) sets stage for new AI chips at its annual tech event. (01:13) Jobs, CPI reports for October may never be released - White House. (01:45)

This is an abridged transcript.

Alibaba Group Holding (BABA) is reportedly preparing an overhaul of its main mobile AI app in the coming months to help it more closely resemble OpenAI’s (OPENAI) ChatGPT.

Bloomberg News reported that Alibaba (BABA) plans to start by updating the existing “Tongyi” apps on iOS and Android and renaming them “Qwen,” after the company’s well-known AI model.

The report added, it will then gradually add agentic-AI features in the coming months to support shopping on platforms, including the main marketplace Taobao.

The end goal is to try to make Qwen a fully functioning AI agent. Alibaba plans to eventually expand globally with an overseas version, and has dedicated more than 100 developers from around the company to the makeover over the past months, as part of additional investments in AI.

Sticking with the topic of AI.

Baidu (BIDU) unveiled two new chips for AI processors and supercomputing products at its annual flagship technology conference, themed 'AI in Action.'

According to a Reuters report, the M100—an inference-focused chip—is set to be launched in early 2026, while the M300, capable of both training and inference, is slated for early 2027.

The move supports China's broader strategy to create a robust domestic tech stack independent of foreign chip suppliers.

The government is open again.

“And it's an honor now to sign this incredible bill and get our country working again.”

That was President Trump late on Wednesday signing into law a federal spending package after it was cleared by Congress, ending the longest shutdown in U.S. history.

But even as the government shutdown ends, the White House is saying some data may be lost forever.

Here’s White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt:

“...with October cpi and jobs reports likely never being released and all of that economic data release will be permanently impaired leaving our policy makers at the Fed flying blind at a critical period.”

Both pieces of audio courtesy of the White House YouTube channel.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics' employees were furloughed during the shutdown. The agency temporarily recalled some workers to release the September CPI report in time to set the Social Security cost-of-living-adjustment for 2026.

The BLS is expected to publish the September jobs report, since the relevant data had been collected before the shutdown began.

But releasing the reports for October is a bigger challenge, as retroactively collecting data could lead to inaccuracies due to recall bias.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

Goldman's AI layoffs early warning signal

Weight Watchers plans to sell Novo's obesity pill once it launches - report

Waymo launches autonomous freeway rides in San Francisco, Los Angeles, Phoenix

Catalyst watch:

  • Bausch & Lomb (BLCO) investor day.

  • Planet Fitness investor day.

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 0.4% at $58/barrel. Bitcoin is up 1.4% at $103,000. Gold is up 0.9% at $4,232.

The FTSE 100 is down 0.4% and the DAX is down 0.5%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Cisco (CSCO) +7% - Shares jumped after the tech giant posted FQ1 results above expectations and raised its full-year outlook.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 12:15 pm St. Louis Federal Reserve Bank President Alberto Musalem will speak on the U.S. economy and monetary policy before the Evansville Regional Economic Partnership 2025 Economic Impact and Policy Forum.

  • 12:20 pm Cleveland Federal Reserve Bank President Beth Hammack will participate in a fireside chat before the Economic Club of Pittsburgh.

data.title: The Market Is Missing UiPath's AI Reversal (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I'm Upgrading My Rating On UiPath Stock

I covered UiPath (PATH) stock only once, when it was a $10-billion market cap company 2 years ago, and I had a thesis that, although its end market's growth prospects look tempting, and the company was likely to beat its then-upcoming earnings at the time, the valuation didn't make sense to me at all. That's why I was neutral despite the rise in AI hopes in the market, and as I can see now, my thesis has played out well:

But today, I think the fundamental setup has flipped, and the building of agentic automation momentum that UiPath is experiencing at its core is exactly what's needed for UiPath's growth re-acceleration. The latest earnings revisions look very bullish to me as well. The stock isn't as expensive as it used to be 2 years ago, and on this front, nothing is holding me back from an upgrade.

Why Do I Think So?

UiPath's fiscal Q2 2026 revenues of $362 million were 14% above last year's figure and managed to beat the consensus estimate by over 4%, based on Seeking Alpha data. They added ~$31 million to their ARR, which eventually amounted to $1.723 billion (+11% YoY), as of Q2. The management noted on the call that AI and Agentic solutions helped the company "win deals and increase deal sizes faster than traditional automation engagements", with about 450 customers actively engaged in developing AI agents now and ~1 million agent runs executed since the program's launch.

We continue to acquire high-quality new logos. For new customers, [deterministic] automation remains a high ROI, low barrier entry point. That's why over 95% of new logos this quarter included our core automation capabilities. For our large installed base, our new AI and agentic capabilities are translating into real momentum. Since launching our Agentic platform, customers executed almost 1 million agent runs. Maestro orchestrated over 170,000 process instances, and over 450 customers are actively developing agents.

Source: PATH's earnings call

The number of customers with ARR over $100,000 and over $1 million increased by 12.44% and 9.22% YoY, respectively, and UiPath also kept expanding deployments at Voya Financial and major public sector clients like the U.S. Navy. At the same time, their "dollar-based gross retention remained best-in-class at 98%", with a dollar-based net retention rate at ~108%. The resulting increase in key financial margins like adjusted gross profit margin (84% in Q2 2026 vs. 83% last year), amid the contractions in absolute OPEX figures like S&M and G&A, is exactly what I wanted to see 2 years ago, but what PATH lacked at the time. Now, these fundamentals have finally flipped, in my view:

UiPath's pipeline quality is up, and its key margins are on the rise, which leads to overall better earnings visibility. With just 1% YoY increase in non-GAAP gross margin in Q2 2026, the company's adjusted EBIT went up by a factor of 9.5, which is very bullish. In situations like this, it's becoming hard for analysts to predict the forward earnings because it's virtually impossible to predict the existing operating leverage effect on the bottom line in the coming quarters. Here, the consensus revisions' trajectory becomes the key barometer for assessing the magnitude of future earnings growth. For the past 3 months, the long-term EPS consensus estimates have doubled in size, which coincides with the period after PATH reported its Q2 2026.

For the upcoming quarter, PATH's management guided for $390-395 million in sales, and the non-GAAP EBIT should rise by 12.36% QoQ to $70 million for the quarter. In Q2, they delivered 15 cents in earnings per diluted share (non-GAAP), and it was a beat of almost 80% to the initial market's consensus. I forgot to mention that they repurchased ~8.3 million shares at an average price of $12.10 during Q2 2026 alone. Now, they're effectively guiding for a 12-13% increase in adjusted EBIT, and they keep buying back their shares from the market, and the current consensus is still projecting a QoQ decrease in Q3 2026 EPS of -6.67%:

UiPath reports in 3 weeks, and from the above picture, it looks like they're once again poised to beat the consensus in a meaningful way. I think it's a major catalyst hiding in plain sight for now, which is likely to play out shortly.

Their updated Agentic Automation suite is not only winning new customers but also expanding wallet share among existing clients, which explains the upward revisions in forward earnings expectations - although, in my view, those revisions are still not sufficiently large in size, leaving a lot of room for beating when PATH reports for its Q3.

As I noted at the beginning of the article, I was primarily concerned about PATH's valuation 2 years ago, as it seemed too stretched to me at the time. Then, a fast-paced multiple contraction took place, and PATH lost ~80% of its market cap value in 5 years as the multiple shrank amid decelerating revenue growth momentum:

Now, as I said, the setup is reversing. With the new enterprise AI automation offerings that they're upselling and smart cost management practices in place, PATH's top-line expansion started to gradually improve, and higher margins led to accelerated profitability gains (compared to 2024).

UiPath isn't a fresh high-growth stock from the AI world, so I'd not expect it to trade at 10-15x forward sales tomorrow. But what I think may be fair at PATH's prospective earnings growth today is a 25-26x earnings multiple next year. The company's sector has a median FWD P/E of 24x, and PATH's growth rates look superior to that sector in general, so there should be a logical valuation premium if that growth lasts for the next few years.

In FY2024 and FY2025, PATH beat the annual EPS consensus by 13.67% and 13.03%, respectively, so I assume they can once again reach 13% beating this year, and then show a 5-10% beat in FY2027. If my math is right, then FY2027 EPS should look as follows:

FY2026 EPS consensus * Premium * (Consensus FY2027 YoY growth * Premium) = FY2027 EPS

$0.66 * 1.13 * ( 1.1199 * 1.075 ) = $0.8978 (vs. the current consensus of $0.74)

At the above FY2027, and assuming a midpoint of the range I picked (25-26x), I get a 12-month price target of ~$22.9/share. It's an upside of slightly over 60% to the current stock price.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

My EPS estimates may be misleading in nature as they're based on a variety of bullish assumptions, which may never come true in reality. And the main risk to my upgrade today is my core assumption that the management's initiatives around the Agentic Automation area will eventually lead to higher-than-expected earnings growth. In reality, there are a lot of obstacles they'll have to go through to get there, and the execution risks are very high, which should always be kept in mind. That 1 million agent runs PATH managed to achieve looks like a pilot and proof-of-concept activity, still. So, if PATH doesn't scale the way I foresee it, PATH's multiples can easily go much deeper, and that 25-26x P/E range in 12-16 months may never become a reality.

The Bottom Line

I believe that UiPath's prospective recovery is likely as they move quickly on capturing the rise in the AI agentic market and signing new logos. At the same time, they're growing more profitably, and I think that the current consensus is still too pessimistic (especially regarding the bottom line), leaving a lot of room for beating in Q3/Q4 (maybe in FY2027-28 as well). The valuation excessiveness that I saw 2 years ago is gone now.

I'm not a specialist in technical analysis, but from what I see, PATH initially failed to settle close to its nearest resistance zone of $19-20, although it broke through a very important level of the previous local high of ~$16.68. Now, PATH is coming down, and it's near its 200-day moving average. I think it can stall right here for a while, and when the Q3 date comes, the stock is likely to push off that red line (see below) on beating earnings, and the reverse head-and-shoulders pattern will be effectively complete.

Based on all this, I'm upgrading PATH stock from my previous "Hold" rating to a "Buy" today.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Ouster: Physical AI Starting To Shine
data.description:
In an odd move, Ouster, Inc. (OUST) slipped following an inflection point quarter, due in part to the weak overall stock market. The Physical AI company is starting to see a huge benefit from focusing on the non-automotive market with record LiDAR sensor sales. My investment thesis remains ultra bullish on the stock, especially after the recent dip.

Inflection Quarter

The LiDAR sensor company reported the following strong Q3 '25 results:

  • Q3 GAAP EPS of -$0.37 beats by $0.08.
  • Revenue of $39.53 million (+40.8% YoY) beats by $2.46 million.

Ouster reported Q3 sales surged over 40% to reach nearly $40 million. After a tough period where sequential sales were minimal and the company struggled to beat estimates, Ouster is firing on all cylinders now with large quarterly beats and strong growth.

The Physical AI company guided to Q4 revenues of $41.0 million, above the $40.5 million consensus estimates. The stock is down on the news, but another big beat like Q3 leads to Q4'25 revenues of $44 million, up a similar $4.5 million sequentially.

On the Q3'25 earnings call, CEO Charles Pacala outlined how the strong Q3 results were only the tip of the iceberg for LiDAR sensor demand:

...we have over 1,000 end customers. And one of the points that we've made and one of the kind of bright spots about Ouster's promise of the future is that there's a small minority of all of the high-quality customers that we have that have actually reached full-scale production and commercial release of their products that are built with an Ouster LiDAR inside. And so that means there's immense opportunity in our existing latent customer base for tranches of these customers to go from development all the way to commercial lease.

So that kind of fundamental order of magnitude shift is a big part of our growth strategy for the foreseeable future. And a small fraction of our overall customer mix under 10% is actually in that full-scale production.

As a prime example given on the earnings call, a customer like Serve Tech. (SERV) going from 50 delivery robots with a LiDAR sensor to 2,000 this year and likely move towards 5K to 10K in the next couple of years. Serve Tech. has clearly reached production scale, but one can argue the company hasn't approached full-scale production, with analyst Timothy Savageaux from Northland Capital Markets suggesting the Los Angeles food delivery robot opportunity alone reaches 10,000, leaving Serve Tech. with only 20% of the robots needed to serve just one market.

The LiDAR company has clearly seen an inflection point in LiDAR sensor demand with 50% growth in shipments since Q1, but the numbers will have to reach the millions before the story is mainstream. Ouster has reported the following quarterly LiDAR sensor shipments:

  • Q3 2025 - 7,200.
  • Q2 2025 - 5,500.
  • Q1 2025 - 4,700.
  • Q4 2024 - 4,800.
  • Q3 2024 - 3,900.
  • Q2 2024 - 4,000.
  • Q1 2024 - 4,500.
  • Q4 2023 - 4,100.

Still Small

Ouster has seen the market cap slip below $1.5 billion with current 2026 consensus revenue estimates of only $200 million. The stock trades at about 7.5x 2026 sales target while the targets still don't reflect a major inflection point in demand.

The consensus estimates forecast Ouster only growing in the 35% to 40% range over the next couple of years. The company would only hit 2027 sales of $280 million, but major customers like Serve Tech. are going through inflection points where orders will 10x, or more.

The best part of Ouster is flipping the script on large losses by LiDAR companies, especially those still chasing the automotive market. The company only reported a minimal Q3 adjusted EBITDA loss of $10 million and has just burned ~$25 million in cash for the YTD period while ending September with a cash balance of $247 million.

Ouster is working on finishing the new L4 and Chronos custom chips to double the TAM. The company is poised to have an automotive grade LiDAR sensor, but the auto manufacturers remain slow to adopt LiDAR with the main auto demand coming from robotaxis, where a lot of the main competitors build their own Lidars.

The company has wisely targeted non-automotive markets, such as the $19 billion smart infrastructure opportunity. The scale of these LiDAR markets provides support for Ouster topping current consensus estimates as more and more of the 1,000+ customer base shifts to production scale orders.

The stock remains attractively priced based on current analyst estimates, but the big opportunity is for growth rates to accelerate. Ouster has an opportunity with autonomous drones in the military with the Department of Defense approval for use in unmanned aerial systems under the Blue UAS Framework.

Ouster has so many opportunities to scale far beyond the current analyst estimates, making the stock appealing with such a minimal market cap of $1.5 billion.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Ouster has now fallen some $17 from the recent highs while the company reported strong Q3 results. Investors should use the dip to load up on a leading physical AI company heading into a major growth inflection.
